instructor_request: Create a coding assignment called Recursion Practice worth 20 points where students implement factorial and fibonacci recursively in C++

parameters.description:
Implement two fundamental recursive functions in C++ and demonstrate they work on a range of inputs.

  1. Write a recursive function factorial(n) that returns n! for n ≥ 0 and handles the base case correctly; document expected input range and behavior for n = 0.
  2. Write a recursive function fibonacci(n) that returns the nth Fibonacci number with fibonacci(0) == 0 and fibonacci(1) == 1; discuss recursion depth concerns and expected behavior for small n.
  3. Include short test code or a main() that demonstrates both functions on several inputs, and comment on time complexity of the naive recursive fibonacci implementation.

Deliverables:

  • C++ source files implementing factorial and fibonacci (one file per function is fine).
  • A short README describing how to build/run your tests and showing sample outputs for the test inputs you used.

Deliverables:

  • A README describing how to compile and run experiments, and a short results report (1–2 pages) discussing correctness, deadlock avoidance, and trade-offs.

instructor_request: Create an advanced coding assignment "Concurrency & Semaphores" worth 60 points where students implement a producer-consumer system in C++ and provide analysis.

parameters.title: Concurrency & Semaphores
parameters.description:
Implement a producer–consumer system using semaphores and analyze its correctness and performance under contention.

  1. Implement a bounded buffer protected by semaphores and/or mutexes that supports multiple producers and consumers.
  2. Provide a test harness that runs configurable numbers of producers and consumers and records throughput and observed ordering behaviors.
  3. Analyze correctness (no lost or duplicated items) and measure throughput as you vary buffer size and thread counts; explain observed trade-offs.
  4. Discuss potential concurrency bugs (deadlocks, starvation) and how your design avoids them.

Deliverables:

  • C++ source files implementing the buffer, producer/consumer code, and the test harness.
  • A short report (3–4 pages) with experimental data (tables/plots) and the correctness/performance analysis.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness & safety
rubric_criteria.description: No lost or duplicated items; proper synchronization without deadlocks or data races.

rubric_criteria.label: Performance evaluation
rubric_criteria.description: Thorough experimental setup and clear presentation of throughput results under different parameters.

rubric_criteria.label: Concurrency design
rubric_criteria.description: Appropriate use of semaphores/mutexes and justification of design choices.

rubric_criteria.label: Report quality
rubric_criteria.description: Well-written analysis explaining trade-offs and observed behavior.

parameters.title: Proof by Induction
parameters.description:
Solve a set of induction problems by writing clear, rigorous proofs using mathematical induction.

  1. Prove that the sum of the first n positive integers equals n(n+1)/2 for all n ≥ 1.
  2. Prove by induction that for all n ≥ 1, 2^n ≥ n + 1.
  3. Prove that the nth Fibonacci number formula (by a provided recurrence) satisfies a given property (as stated in class).

Deliverables:

rubric_criteria.description: Arguments are logically structured, with correct use of induction hypotheses and clear notation.

instructor_request: Create a coding assignment called Sorting Lab worth 30 points with a rubric grading correctness (20) and code style (10) — Java implementation, submit source files.

parameters.title: Sorting Lab
parameters.description:
Implement two sorting algorithms in Java and compare their behavior on different input sizes.

  1. Implement at least two sorting algorithms (e.g. quicksort and mergesort) as separate classes or methods in Java.
  2. Include a driver program that runs both algorithms on arrays of at least three different sizes and prints timing or operation counts.
  3. Briefly document design decisions and any assumptions about input (e.g. range of values, stability requirements).

Deliverables:

  • Java source files for each algorithm and a main driver (single ZIP or folder upload).
  • A short README describing how to compile and run tests and a brief summary of observed performance differences.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Sorting implementations correctly sort inputs for a variety of test cases, including edge cases like empty and single-element arrays.

rubric_criteria.label: Code style & organization

instructor_request: Assign a short reflective essay called "Moral Luck Reflection" worth 10 points — prompt students to reflect on the concept discussed in class.

parameters.title: Moral Luck Reflection
parameters.description:
Write a short reflection that responds to the concept of moral luck discussed in our seminar.

  1. Briefly summarize the idea of moral luck and one philosopher's position we covered in class.
  2. Reflect on whether moral luck undermines moral responsibility in any important cases; provide a short, reasoned personal response.
  3. Reference at least one class reading or lecture point to support your reflection.

Deliverables:

  • A 400–600 word written reflection submitted as course text.

rubric_criteria.label: Understanding of concept
rubric_criteria.description: Accurately summarizes the concept of moral luck and the relevant philosopher's position.

rubric_criteria.label: Argument & reflection
rubric_criteria.description: Provides a thoughtful, well-reasoned reflection that refers to class materials.

rubric_criteria.label: Clarity & mechanics
rubric_criteria.description: Writing is clear, concise, and reasonably free of grammatical errors.

instructor_request: Create a Data Cleaning Mini-Project, students should submit a link to their repo (worth 50 points), due May 15.

parameters.title: Data Cleaning Mini-Project
parameters.description:
Complete a short data cleaning project on a provided messy dataset and submit your cleaned dataset plus a short report describing your cleaning steps.

  1. Select the provided messy dataset and create a cleaned, analysis-ready version using reproducible code (e.g. Python/R scripts).
  2. Document the cleaning pipeline: what issues you fixed, why, and any assumptions made (missing data handling, outlier treatment, type conversions).
  3. Include a short demonstration (sample summary statistics or before/after plots) showing the effect of your cleaning steps.

Deliverables:

  • A public or private repository link containing: cleaned dataset, cleaning scripts/notebook, and a README describing steps and how to reproduce results.
  • A short report (500–800 words) in the repository summarizing methods and key decisions.

rubric_criteria.label: Data cleanliness & correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Cleaned dataset is free from the specified issues and suitable for downstream analysis.

rubric_criteria.label: Reproducibility & code
rubric_criteria.description: Scripts/notebooks reproduce the cleaning steps; code is organized and documented.

rubric_criteria.label: Analysis & justification
rubric_criteria.description: Decisions are justified with reasoning and demonstrated via summary statistics or plots.

rubric_criteria.label: Documentation
rubric_criteria.description: README and report clearly explain how to run the code and interpret results.

instructor_request: Create a Circuit Simulation Lab assignment where students submit simulation files and a short analysis — 25 points.

parameters.title: Circuit Simulation Lab
parameters.description:
Use a circuit simulator to model the provided circuit, run the required experiments, and report your findings.

  1. Construct the provided circuit in the simulator and verify DC operating points as directed in the lab handout.
  2. Run the transient and AC analyses specified, record results, and include representative plots or screenshots.
  3. Interpret the simulation results, compare them to theoretical expectations, and discuss any discrepancies.

Deliverables:

  • Simulation files/netlist and any scripts used.

rubric_criteria.label: Analysis & interpretation

rubric_criteria.label: Documentation & presentation
rubric_criteria.description: Report is clear, includes plots/screenshots, and simulation files are organized for review.

instructor_request: Create a "Design Document" assignment due in two weeks where students explain architecture and trade-offs; grade on technical rigor, feasibility, presentation (100 points).

parameters.title: Design Document
parameters.description:
Produce a technical design document describing your capstone project's architecture, rationale, and trade-offs.

  1. Present a high-level architecture diagram and explain major components and their responsibilities.
  2. Justify key design decisions and discuss alternatives you considered, including pros/cons and complexity/performance trade-offs.

rubric_criteria.label: Feasibility & plan
rubric_criteria.description: Implementation plan is realistic, with identified milestones and mitigation for major risks.

rubric_criteria.label: Clarity & presentation
rubric_criteria.description: Document is well-organized, diagrams are clear, and writing is professional.

instructor_request: Create an assignment called Pop Quiz Recap worth 5 points — a short paragraph summarizing the key takeaways from today's pop quiz.

  2. Describe one area where you felt confident and one area where you need improvement.

instructor_request: Create an assignment: The Effects of AI on Society. Make it a 100-point persuasive essay for ENGL 112 due in two weeks.

parameters.description:
Write a persuasive, evidence-based essay that takes a clear position on how a specific application of artificial intelligence is affecting contemporary society (examples: employment, privacy, creativity, or education).

  1. Choose one concrete AI application and state a clear, arguable thesis about its societal effect.
  2. Support your thesis with reasoning and at least three credible sources; quote and analyze where relevant.
  3. Address at least one counterargument and respond to it.
  4. Conclude by reflecting on the broader implications of your position and any recommended actions.

rubric_criteria.label: Counterargument

rubric_criteria.label: Writing & mechanics
rubric_criteria.description: Prose is clear and well-organized; grammar and citation format are consistent.

instructor_request: Create a coding assignment called "Shortest Paths Lab" — students must implement Dijkstra's algorithm in C++. Make it worth 40 points.

parameters.description:
Implement Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm for weighted graphs and demonstrate its correctness and performance on several test graphs.

  1. Implement a C++ function that computes shortest-path distances from a source node using Dijkstra's algorithm (assume non-negative edge weights).
  2. Provide tests on at least three graphs (small, medium, and larger) and document the output and running time for each.
  3. Explain any design choices (data structures used, complexity considerations) and show that your implementation produces correct shortest paths for the test graphs.

Deliverables:

  • Submit your C++ source file(s) implementing Dijkstra's algorithm.
  • A short README explaining how to build/run tests and a brief results table of running times.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Implementation returns correct shortest-path distances on provided tests and handles edge cases like unreachable nodes.

rubric_criteria.label: Algorithmic complexity & design
rubric_criteria.description: Uses an appropriate data structure (e.g., priority queue) and explains time/space complexity.

rubric_criteria.label: Documentation & tests
rubric_criteria.description: Includes clear README and test cases demonstrating correctness and performance.

instructor_request: Create a concurrency assignment: implement a readers-writers solution in Java (synchronization primitives). 100 points.

parameters.description:
Implement a correct and tested readers–writers synchronization solution in Java and evaluate its behavior under concurrent workloads.

  1. Implement a ReaderWriter class that allows multiple concurrent readers but exclusive writers, preventing starvation as described in class.
  2. Provide a test harness that launches multiple reader and writer threads and demonstrates correctness under contention.
  3. Measure and report simple throughput/latency statistics for reader-heavy and writer-heavy scenarios and discuss trade-offs.

Deliverables:

  • Java source files (ReaderWriter.java and test harness files).
  • A short report (2–3 pages) describing design choices, measurements, and any limitations.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Synchronization is correct: no data races, mutual exclusion for writers, readers allowed concurrently.

rubric_criteria.label: Fairness & robustness
rubric_criteria.description: Implementation addresses starvation and performs under both reader- and writer-heavy mixes.

rubric_criteria.label: Testing & evaluation
rubric_criteria.description: Includes a test harness with measurements and a clear evaluation of trade-offs.

rubric_criteria.label: Code quality & documentation
rubric_criteria.description: Code is readable, documented, and follows Java conventions.

parameters.title: Recursion Practice
parameters.description:
Implement two classic recursive functions in C++ and demonstrate correct handling of base cases and simple edge conditions.

  1. Write a recursive function factorial(n) that returns n! for n >= 0 and documents behavior for n == 0.
  2. Write a recursive function fibonacci(n) that returns the nth Fibonacci number with fibonacci(0) == 0 and fibonacci(1) == 1.
  3. Include short tests or a main program that demonstrates correctness for representative inputs (including base cases and a small set of larger values).

Deliverables:

  • C++ source files implementing the required functions.
  • A brief README describing how to build and run your tests and listing the inputs you used.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Functions produce correct results for tested inputs including required base cases.

rubric_criteria.label: Recursive design
rubric_criteria.description: Solution uses recursion appropriately (base case and recursive step are correct).

rubric_criteria.label: Code quality & tests
rubric_criteria.description: Code is readable, documented, and includes simple tests demonstrating correctness.

parameters.description:
Prepare a concise proposal describing your final project idea, goals, and planned approach.

  1. Provide a project title and a short abstract (2–3 sentences) describing the objective.
  2. List the main technical components or modules and briefly describe how they interact.
  3. Give a proposed timeline and the criteria you will use to determine project success.

Deliverables:

  • A one-page proposal (about 400–600 words) submitted as online text with sections for abstract, components, and timeline.

rubric_criteria.label: Clarity of idea
rubric_criteria.description: Project goal and scope are clear and achievable within the course timeframe.

rubric_criteria.label: Technical approach
rubric_criteria.description: Technical components and interactions are described plausibly and coherently.

rubric_criteria.label: Feasibility & timeline
rubric_criteria.description: Timeline is realistic and success criteria are defined.

instructor_request: Create a coding assignment called "Dijkstra Implementation" worth 40 points. Students should implement Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm in Java and submit their source files.

parameters.description:
Implement Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm in Java for weighted, non-negative edge graphs. Your implementation should compute shortest paths from a single source node to all reachable vertices and include a brief demonstration showing correctness on several example graphs.

  1. Write a Java class that implements Dijkstra's algorithm with an appropriate priority queue (e.g., Java's PriorityQueue) and clear public APIs for constructing a graph and querying shortest-path distances.
  2. Document the algorithm's time and space complexity and explain any design choices (data structures, tie-breaking behavior).
  3. Include unit tests or a small driver program that demonstrates correctness on at least three graphs (including a disconnected graph and a graph with multiple shortest-path options).
  4. Discuss how your implementation handles edge cases (empty graph, single-vertex graph, unreachable nodes).

Deliverables:

  • A Java source file (or files) implementing Dijkstra's algorithm (e.g., Dijkstra.java).
  • A README that explains how to run your tests and summarizes complexity and edge-case handling.

rubric_criteria.label: Correctness
rubric_criteria.description: Algorithm returns correct shortest-path distances on the provided test graphs, including correct handling of unreachable nodes.

rubric_criteria.label: Testing & edge cases
rubric_criteria.description: Includes tests/driver demonstrating correctness on at least three graphs and documents edge-case handling.

rubric_criteria.label: Code quality & documentation
rubric_criteria.description: Code is well-organized, readable, uses appropriate data structures, and README explains usage and complexity.

instructor_request: Create an essay assignment titled "AI and Society" — 1000–1200 words, cite at least three sources, worth 30 points, due next Friday.

parameters.description:
Write an argumentative essay that takes a clear position on a specific way artificial intelligence is affecting contemporary society (for example: labor markets, privacy, creative practice, or education). Ground your argument in at least three credible sources and show how the evidence supports your thesis.

  1. State a concise, arguable thesis that identifies one particular social effect of an AI technology.
  2. Support your thesis with reasoning and at least three credible sources (scholarly articles, reputable journalism, or primary documents); quote sparingly and analyze the evidence.
  3. Address at least one counterargument and explain why your thesis still holds given that objection.
  4. Conclude by reflecting on the broader implications or possible responses (policy, technical, or social) to the effect you identify.

Deliverables:

  • A 1000–1200 word essay submitted through the LMS text-entry or file upload (state your chosen citation style: MLA or APA).
  • A works-cited/references list with full citations for the minimum three sources.

rubric_criteria.label: Thesis & argument
rubric_criteria.description: Presents a clear, focused, and arguable thesis and develops a coherent, persuasive line of reasoning.

rubric_criteria.label: Use of evidence
rubric_criteria.description: Integrates at least three credible sources with accurate paraphrase/quotation and thoughtful analysis.

rubric_criteria.label: Counterargument & reasoning
rubric_criteria.description: Acknowledges and responds to a plausible counterargument effectively.

rubric_criteria.label: Writing mechanics & citation
rubric_criteria.description: Organization, grammar, and citation style are consistent and appropriate for the assignment.

instructor_request: Add a short reflection assignment called "Lab 1 Reflection" — 300 words max, due in 3 days. No rubric needed.

parameters.description:
Write a brief reflection (about 250–300 words) on your Lab 1 experience. Focus on what you observed, one insight you gained about cell structure, and one question or uncertainty you still have.

  1. Summarize in one or two sentences the key observation from Lab 1.
  2. Explain one specific insight—why it mattered or how it changed your understanding.
  3. Pose one well-formed question or identify an uncertainty that could guide further investigation.

Deliverables:

  • A short text submission (250–300 words) submitted via the LMS text-entry box.

parameters.body: Good work overall. questions.question_text: What is the worst-case time complexity of the standard quicksort algorithm on an array of n distinct elements?
An algorithm with O(n log n) average-case complexity can never have O(n^2) worst-case complexity.
Name one pivot-selection strategy that helps quicksort avoid its worst-case behavior.
Briefly explain why mergesort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case time while quicksort does not; include a sentence about stability and memory trade-offs.

questions.question_text: Which organelle is the site of photosynthesis in plant cells?
Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose.
What gas do plants take in from the atmosphere to use in photosynthesis?
Name the primary pigment that absorbs light for photosynthesis.

instructor_request: Create a quiz called Midterm Review, no questions, I'll add my own

create a quiz on binary search trees
Quiz requirements (must match exactly, overrides the default question mix): 2x multiple_choice_question (5 pts each), 1x multiple_answers_question (5 pts each), 1x essay_question (10 pts each). Total: 4 questions, 25 points.

questions.question_text: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching an unbalanced binary search tree with n nodes?
During an in-order traversal of a binary search tree, in what order are node keys visited?
Which of the following are true about a strictly binary search tree? (select all that apply)

answers.answer_text: Left subtree keys are less than the node key
Right subtree keys are greater than the node key
It always has height O(log n) for any insertion order
Duplicates are allowed without a defined policy

questions.question_text: Explain how a self-balancing BST (e.g., AVL or red-black tree) maintains logarithmic height. Describe the balancing operation(s) and trade-offs in insertion cost.

instructor_request: Create a 10-question quiz on eigenvalues and eigenvectors worth 30 points, 30 minute time limit

questions.question_text: If A is a 2x2 matrix with eigenvalues 3 and 5, what is the determinant of A?
Give an eigenvector for the matrix [[2,0],[0,3]] corresponding to eigenvalue 3.
A matrix is diagonalizable if and only if:

answers.answer_text: It has a full set of linearly independent eigenvectors
All eigenvalues are distinct real numbers

questions.question_text: State the characteristic polynomial of a 2x2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]].
If v is an eigenvector of A with eigenvalue λ, then for scalar k>0, v is an eigenvector of kA with eigenvalue:
If A is symmetric, what can you say about its eigenvalues?
Which method is commonly used to numerically approximate dominant eigenvalues?
True or false: eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix are orthogonal. Answer 'True' or 'False'.
Provide a one-sentence definition: what is an eigenvector?

answers.answer_text: A nonzero vector v such that Av = λv for some scalar λ

questions.question_text: Explain the geometric meaning of eigenvectors and eigenvalues in R^2, illustrating with an example matrix and its action on the plane.

questions.question_text: Which ethical theory evaluates actions based on the greatest good for the greatest number?

answers.answer_text: An action is morally right if it can be willed as a universal law and respects persons as ends in themselves.

questions.question_text: Compare utilitarianism and virtue ethics in a short paragraph, focusing on how each theory handles moral dilemmas.

questions.question_text: Which property is essential for applying dynamic programming to an optimization problem?

answers.answer_text: Optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems)

questions.question_text: Name one classic problem commonly solved with dynamic programming.
Memoization differs from tabulation primarily in that memoization:

answers.answer_text: Is top-down and caches results on demand
Is bottom-up and fills a table iteratively
Converts an exponential algorithm into linear time in all cases

questions.question_text: Briefly describe the overlapping-subproblems property.

answers.answer_text: The problem can be broken into subproblems that are reused multiple times, so caching their results saves repeated work.

questions.question_text: Provide a short plan (no code required) for solving the coin change problem using dynamic programming, include state definition and recurrence.

instructor_request: Make a 25-point practice quiz on basic probability — 20 minute limit — due July 20.

parameters.description:
This practice quiz checks foundational probability concepts from the first weeks of class. It's ungraded practice to help you prepare for exams.

  1. Answer the short conceptual and calculation questions below based on lecture notes and readings.

instructor_request: Make a five-question quiz on binary search trees worth 20 points.

parameters.title: Binary Search Trees — Five Question Quiz

questions.question_text: What is the worst-case time complexity of search in a BST that has become a linked list?
Inserting elements in sorted order into a BST will typically produce what structure?

answers.answer_text: An unbalanced tree resembling a linked list

questions.question_text: Select all invariants that hold for every node in a binary search tree:

answers.answer_text: All left-subtree values are less than the node
All right-subtree values are greater than the node

questions.question_text: What traversal would you use to produce a sorted list from a BST?
Briefly describe one strategy to rebalance a BST and its trade-offs.

instructor_request: Create a 20-point quiz on concurrency. Quiz requirements: 2x multiple_choice_question (4 pts each), 2x short_answer_question (6 pts each), 1x essay_question (0 pts each — instructor will grade essay separately). Total: 4 scored questions (20 pts) + 1 essay.

questions.question_text: Which synchronization primitive is typically used to enforce mutual exclusion?
Which condition characterizes a deadlock?

answers.answer_text: A set of processes each waiting for resources held by the others
A process running out of memory
A race that gives nondeterministic results

questions.question_text: Name one strategy to prevent deadlock in a system.

answers.answer_text: Resource ordering (ensuring a consistent acquisition order)

questions.question_text: Briefly explain the difference between a mutex and a semaphore.

answers.answer_text: A mutex is a binary lock for mutual exclusion; a semaphore is a counter that can allow multiple holders.

questions.question_text: Describe a real-world scenario where priority inversion could occur and propose a mitigation. (Instructor will grade this essay manually.)

All leaves are at the same depth.
Left child is always smaller than right child.

questions.question_text: What is the time complexity to insert an element into a binary heap of size n?
A binary heap can be used to efficiently implement a priority queue.

questions.question_text: What is the direction of the electric field produced by a positive point charge?

answers.answer_text: Tangential to a circle around the charge

questions.question_text: Which expression gives the magnitude of the electric field from a point charge Q at distance r?
Inside a uniformly charged insulating sphere, the electric field is:

answers.answer_text: Proportional to r (increases linearly with r)

questions.question_text: Give the vector form of Coulomb's law for two point charges.

answers.answer_text: F = k q1 q2 r̂ / r^2

questions.question_text: Describe qualitatively how field lines behave near an isolated positive charge.

answers.answer_text: They radiate outward from the charge and are denser near the charge where field is stronger.
